title: Trauma and the Proprioceptive System: Body Awareness and Trauma Recovery
excerpt: Explore how trauma impacts the proprioceptive system and how body awareness supports trauma recovery and nervous system regulation.
body:
Understanding Trauma Beyond Memory: The Body’s Role

For many people who have undergone traditional talk therapy, trauma can still feel like an invisible weight lodged deep within the body. This isn’t surprising. Pioneers in trauma research like Bessel van der Kolk have emphasized that trauma isn’t just a psychological event—it’s deeply embodied. One crucial but often overlooked system involved in trauma recovery is the proprioceptive system, our internal sense of body position and movement.

What Is the Proprioceptive System?

Proprioception is sometimes called the “sixth sense.” It’s our body’s ability to sense where it is in space without looking. This system integrates input from muscles, joints, and tendons, sending constant feedback to the brain about posture, movement, and force. This feedback helps us coordinate actions, maintain balance, and regulate muscle tone.

Unlike the more familiar five senses, proprioception is mostly unconscious but foundational to how we experience ourselves physically. When it’s working well, we move smoothly and feel grounded. When it’s disrupted, the body can feel disconnected, uncoordinated, or unsafe.

Trauma’s Impact on the Proprioceptive System

Trauma, especially when overwhelming or repeated, can disrupt proprioceptive processing. Peter Levine, developer of Somatic Experiencing, outlines how trauma floods the nervous system, triggering freeze, fight, or flight responses. These states alter muscle tone and disrupt normal sensory feedback loops.

For example, in a freeze response, muscles may become rigid or immobilized, blocking movement and proprioceptive input. This can cause a person to feel ‘stuck’ in their body or dissociated from sensations.

Richard Schwartz’s Internal Family Systems model also underscores how trauma fragments the self. Parts of the body and mind may become out of sync, with some ‘exiled’ parts holding trauma memories disconnected from conscious awareness. Proprioceptive disruption can contribute to this fragmentation.

Why Body Awareness Matters in Trauma Recovery

Deb Dana, a key clinician applying Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory to trauma therapy, emphasizes the importance of nervous system regulation through body awareness. The proprioceptive system offers a pathway back to safety and integration.

When clients learn to notice their body’s position, movement, and muscle tone, they gain access to information that can calm the nervous system. This somatic awareness helps shift the body out of defensive states and into a window of tolerance where healing can occur.

Pat Ogden, founder of Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, highlights that trauma recovery isn’t just about talking but about re-educating the body’s sensory and motor systems. Proprioceptive input is central to this re-education, helping restore a sense of agency and embodiment.

Scientific Foundations: The Neuroscience of Proprioception and Trauma

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory provides a neurophysiological framework connecting the autonomic nervous system with trauma responses. The vagus nerve, which regulates parasympathetic tone, also modulates proprioceptive feedback through connections with the spinal cord and brainstem.

When trauma triggers defensive autonomic states, proprioceptive signals can become distorted or suppressed. This contributes to symptoms like chronic pain, dissociation, or body numbness common in trauma survivors.

Neuroscientist Dan Siegel emphasizes integration — linking body sensations with emotions and cognition — as essential for trauma recovery. Proprioceptive awareness acts as a bridge between bodily states and conscious experience, allowing for greater self-regulation and coherence.

Practical Approaches to Engaging the Proprioceptive System

For those doing self-directed healing work, engaging the proprioceptive system can be a powerful tool. Here are some evidence-based strategies:

  • Slow, mindful movement: Gentle yoga, tai chi, or qigong encourage proprioceptive input and nervous system regulation. Pat Ogden’s Sensorimotor approach often uses these movements to rewire trauma responses.
  • Weight-bearing exercises: Activities like walking, carrying weighted objects, or pushing against resistance stimulate proprioceptors in muscles and joints, helping ground the nervous system.
  • Body scanning and tracking: Mindfulness practices that focus attention on body position and muscle tension enhance proprioceptive awareness.
  • Somatic Experiencing techniques: Developed by Peter Levine, these involve tracking subtle sensations and encouraging small, titrated movements to discharge trauma energy safely.
  • Polyvagal-informed regulation: Following Deb Dana’s protocols, alternating between activation and soothing states while paying attention to bodily cues supports proprioceptive integration.

Common Challenges and How to Navigate Them

It’s important to acknowledge that reconnecting with the body can sometimes feel overwhelming. Trauma survivors often report feeling flooded, anxious, or dissociated when tuning into bodily sensations. This is because the proprioceptive system is tightly linked with the autonomic nervous system and emotional memory.

Gabor Maté reminds us that trauma recovery requires compassion and patience. Pushing too hard to feel or move can retraumatize. Instead, it’s about gently expanding the window of tolerance over time.

Working with a skilled somatic therapist can provide guidance and safety. For those on a solo path, starting with small, manageable practices and grounding techniques is key.

The Role of Proprioception in Building Resilience and Embodiment

Ultimately, strengthening proprioceptive awareness rebuilds a foundational sense of self. It fosters embodiment — the subjective experience of living fully in the body. This is where trauma survivors often find profound healing, moving from survival to thriving.

Francine Shapiro’s EMDR therapy also incorporates body awareness to process trauma memories, underscoring the integration of mind and body.

"Trauma recovery isn’t just about changing thoughts, it’s about reclaiming the body’s innate wisdom through sensory awareness and movement." — The Oracle Lover

Conclusion: Reclaiming the Body Through Proprioception

Trauma leaves marks not only on the mind but in the very fabric of the body’s sensory systems. The proprioceptive system’s role in trauma recovery is critical yet often underappreciated. By cultivating body awareness and engaging in practices that support proprioceptive integration, trauma survivors can access new pathways toward regulation, resilience, and wholeness.

The research of van der Kolk, Levine, Porges, Dana, Ogden, Maté, Siegel, Schwartz, and Shapiro collectively reveal that healing is a body-mind process. If you’ve felt stuck in your recovery journey, exploring your proprioceptive system might just be the key to unlocking a deeper sense of safety and self-trust.

faqs.q: What is the proprioceptive system and why is it important in trauma recovery?
faqs.a: The proprioceptive system is our internal sense of body position and movement. It helps regulate muscle tone and body awareness, which are often disrupted by trauma, making it crucial for nervous system regulation and healing.

faqs.q: How does trauma affect body awareness?
faqs.a: Trauma can cause the body to freeze or dissociate, disrupting normal proprioceptive signals. This leads to feelings of disconnection from the body, making it harder to regulate emotions and physical responses.

faqs.q: Can I work on proprioceptive awareness without a therapist?
faqs.a: Yes, self-directed practices like mindful movement, body scanning, and gentle weight-bearing exercises can help. However, it’s important to proceed slowly and compassionately to avoid overwhelm.

faqs.q: What role does the autonomic nervous system play in proprioceptive disruption?
faqs.a: The autonomic nervous system governs fight, flight, and freeze responses. Trauma triggers dysregulation here, which can distort proprioceptive feedback and contribute to symptoms like chronic pain or numbness.
